creator: agh@riseup.net
text:
(In reply to Charlie Li from comment #76)
> Matches the specified order in pyproject.toml [core]. I don't think upstream necessarily intentionally specified them like such but having our Makefile match their order makes for easier maintenance as they add or remove dependencies, or when certain ones switch away from setuptools as their build backend.

I do not see how that is a problem when we have port tools that, to some degree, codify the policies of the Ports tree. The Port's tools, as far as I know, do not implement the upstream policies of Python projects; these days, our development environments, with minimal keystrokes make it easy to select, yank, and sort lines in a Port Makefile, or an upstream pyproject.toml. The Porter's Handbook, mentions the various tools[1][2][3], contributors can, and recommends using before updating a Port, or submitting a new Port.

It is consistently boring when committers covertly demand that other contributors read their minds on how, or why a committer has implemented a personal policy within a Port. Honestly, if I had mind reading capacities, FreeBSD committers' would be the last people in the Universe whose mind I would want to read, in fact, in this hypothetical, I would rather make my exit than read the minds of FreeBSD committers.

Please reduce the friction contributors have to heal with; the aims of the Project are greater than the ego of any one, or many committers.

To good health.

1: https://docs.freebsd.org/en/books/porters-handbook/book/#testing-portlint
2: https://docs.freebsd.org/en/books/porters-handbook/book/#testing-portclippy
3: https://docs.freebsd.org/en/books/porters-handbook/book/#testing-porttools
